Read dev/ROADMAP.md design rationale behind v1.0.0 reposition — documents v0.1.x decisions rejected . theoretical lineage package — Shewhart (1926, 1939), Perla/Provost et al. (2020), Ferraz et al. (2020) — summarised references/REFERENCIAL-TEORICO.md (Portuguese), reference mapped specific files/functions. title: ARL by Monte Carlo simulation
text: simplest setup — Nelson 1 , normal data, 3-sigma limits — ARL known closed form. probability falling outside 3-sigma 2⋅Φ(−3)≈0.00272 \cdot \Phi(-3) \approx 0.0027, : ARL0=10.0027≈370.4. \mathrm{ARL}_0 = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370.4. Let’s verify simulation: estimate close 370 (Monte Carlo error around closed form).
code: set.seed(2025) shewhart_arl(   shift = 0,   rules = "nelson_1_beyond_3s",   n_sim = 5000,   max_run = 2000 )

title: ARL by Monte Carlo simulation
text: happens add Nelson 2 (9 points side) top? ARL_0 drops ~370 ~250, meaning false alarms become common. Whether ’s worth depends get detection power. Let’s plot ARL across grid shifts: gain largest small shifts (around 0.5-1 sigma), Nelson 1 alone takes long time fire. shifts 2 sigma , rule sets detect within couple samples.
code: set.seed(2025) shewhart_arl(   shift = 0,   rules = c("nelson_1_beyond_3s", "nelson_2_nine_same"),   n_sim = 5000 ) shifts <- seq(0, 3, by = 0.25)  set.seed(2025) arl_n1 <- shewhart_arl(shifts, "nelson_1_beyond_3s", n_sim = 2000) arl_n12 <- shewhart_arl(shifts,                         c("nelson_1_beyond_3s", "nelson_2_nine_same"),                         n_sim = 2000)  bind_rows(   arl_n1  |> mutate(rules = "Nelson 1 only"),   arl_n12 |> mutate(rules = "Nelson 1 + 2") ) |>   ggplot(aes(shift, arl, colour = rules)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = arl_lower, ymax = arl_upper, fill = rules),               alpha = 0.12, colour = NA) +   scale_y_log10() +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c(`Nelson 1 only` = unname(shewhart_palette("signal")["in_control"]),                `Nelson 1 + 2`  = unname(shewhart_palette("family")["memory_based"]))) +   scale_fill_manual(     values = c(`Nelson 1 only` = unname(shewhart_palette("signal")["in_control"]),                `Nelson 1 + 2`  = unname(shewhart_palette("family")["memory_based"]))) +   labs(x = "Shift (sigma)", y = "ARL (log scale)",        title = "Operating characteristics") +   shewhart_theme()

what: A quantitative comparison: WE-7 vs Nelson 2
title: ARL by Monte Carlo simulation
text: original Shewhart package (now shewhartr) (v0.1.x) used Western Electric “7 row” rule phase detection. default v1.0 Nelson 2 (9 row). trade-: rule’s ARL_0 around 64 (false phase change every 64 -control samples average); Nelson 2’s around 256 (one every ~256 samples). monitoring contexts meaningful difference. rule easier teach, false-alarm cost high. shewhart_regression() accepts either via phase_rule argument:
code: set.seed(2025) arl_we  <- shewhart_arl(0, "we_seven_same",       n_sim = 5000, max_run = 2000) arl_n2  <- shewhart_arl(0, "nelson_2_nine_same",  n_sim = 5000, max_run = 2000) arl_we arl_n2 shewhart_regression(temperature_drift, value = temp_c, index = minute,                     phase_rule = "we_seven_same")        # legacy default shewhart_regression(temperature_drift, value = temp_c, index = minute,                     phase_rule = "nelson_2_nine_same")    # new default

title: ARL by Monte Carlo simulation
text: shewhart_arl() simulates normal distribution default. Real residuals often non-normal — heavy-tailed, skewed, autocorrelated. closed-form ARL_0 370 Nelson 1 overstates rarely false alarms occur. bootstrap variant (simulating actual residuals) roadmap; , quick check call shewhart_diagnostics(fit) inspect Q-Q ACF panels.

title: Attributes charts: p, np, c, u
text: claims_p records 30 days insurance-claim quality control. day, variable number claims (n) processed count errors (defects) observed. n varies day--day, limits also vary day--day: default limits = "3sigma" uses normal approximation p‾±3p‾(1−p‾)/ni\bar p \pm 3\sqrt{\bar p (1 - \bar p)/n_i}. fine nip‾≳5n_i \bar p \gtrsim 5 ni(1−p‾)≳5n_i (1-\bar p) \gtrsim 5. small nn extreme p‾\bar p, switch exact binomial limits:
code: fit <- shewhart_p(claims_p, defects = defects, n = n, index = day) broom::tidy(fit) #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   chart line    value note            #>   <chr> <chr>   <dbl> <chr>           #> 1 p     CL     0.0601 ""              #> 2 p     UCL   NA      "varies with n" #> 3 p     LCL   NA      "varies with n" broom::augment(fit) |> head(10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 11 #>      day  .obs .defects    .n .value .center .sigma .upper .lower #>    <int> <int>    <int> <int>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> #>  1     1     1        7   134 0.0522  0.0601 0.0205  0.122      0 #>  2     2     2        8   140 0.0571  0.0601 0.0201  0.120      0 #>  3     3     3        6   129 0.0465  0.0601 0.0209  0.123      0 #>  4     4     4        3   100 0.03    0.0601 0.0238  0.131      0 #>  5     5     5        6   105 0.0571  0.0601 0.0232  0.130      0 #>  6     6     6        6   109 0.0550  0.0601 0.0228  0.128      0 #>  7     7     7        4    82 0.0488  0.0601 0.0262  0.139      0 #>  8     8     8        3   138 0.0217  0.0601 0.0202  0.121      0 #>  9     9     9        6    90 0.0667  0.0601 0.0251  0.135      0 #> 10    10    10        6   125 0.048   0.0601 0.0213  0.124      0 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: .flag_nelson_1_beyond_3s <lgl>, .flag_any <lgl> shewhart_p(claims_p, defects = defects, n = n, index = day,            limits = "binomial")

title: Attributes charts: p, np, c, u
text: pcb_solder 50 PCBs mean defect count 6. default 3-sigma c-chart works fine : c_bar small (say 2 3), lower limit normal approximation negative — makes sense count. package warns likely: low-mean Poisson processes, use exact quantile limits: George Box’s advice — don’t transform can model right distribution — applies. exact Poisson limits use q(0.99865)q(0.99865) q(0.00135)q(0.00135) Poisson(c‾)\mathrm{Poisson}(\bar c), coverage probability classical 3-sigma limits without normal approximation.
code: fit_c <- shewhart_c(pcb_solder, defects = defects, index = board) #> Warning: c_bar = 5.76 is small; the normal approximation is poor. #> ℹ Consider `limits = "poisson"` for exact limits. broom::tidy(fit_c) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   chart line  value #>   <chr> <chr> <dbl> #> 1 c     CL     5.76 #> 2 c     UCL   13.0  #> 3 c     LCL    0 small_means <- data.frame(unit = 1:50, defects = rpois(50, lambda = 2)) suppressWarnings(   fit_low <- shewhart_c(small_means, defects = defects, index = unit) ) broom::tidy(fit_low) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   chart line  value #>   <chr> <chr> <dbl> #> 1 c     CL     2.02 #> 2 c     UCL    6.28 #> 3 c     LCL    0 fit_low_exact <- shewhart_c(small_means, defects = defects, index = unit,                             limits = "poisson") broom::tidy(fit_low_exact) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   chart line  value #>   <chr> <chr> <dbl> #> 1 c     CL     2.02 #> 2 c     UCL    7    #> 3 c     LCL    0

title: Choosing a transformation: Box-Cox in practice
text: optimal lambda near zero (log transformation), 95% CI cover zero. Let’s plot profile: CI λ\lambda contains 1, transformation needed (data approximately normal ). contains 0, take logs. contains 0.5, take square roots — .
code: set.seed(2025) y <- rlnorm(200, meanlog = 0, sdlog = 0.5)   # log-normal -> lambda = 0 bc <- shewhart_box_cox(y) bc #>  #> ── Box-Cox profile likelihood ────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • n = 200 #> • lambda_hat = 0 #> • 95% CI: [-0.25, 0.2] autoplot(bc)

title: Choosing a transformation: Box-Cox in practice
text: "auto" model shewhart_regression() calls shewhart_box_cox() internally response (+1 shift keep zeros valid) selects among linear, log, loglog according value λ̂\hat \lambda: |λ̂−1|≤0.1|\hat \lambda - 1| \le 0.1 → linear |λ̂−0|≤0.1|\hat \lambda - 0| \le 0.1 → log |λ̂−0.5|≤0.1|\hat \lambda - 0.5| \le 0.1 → loglog otherwise default linear warning guidance step, guarantee. Always inspect residual diagnostics afterwards via shewhart_diagnostics().

title: Choosing a transformation: Box-Cox in practice
text: data counts, proportions, times--event, quantities known parametric family, model family explicitly. Box clear : can model right distribution, . Transforms exist case right distribution isn’t tractable normal approximation suitably-chosen scale best available compromise. c, u, p, np charts package implement advice: support limits = "poisson" ("binomial") exact distribution-aware limits, instead relying transformation coerce counts approximate normality.

what: The right tool: regression chart with phase detection
title: Case study: epidemiological monitoring (COVID-19, Recife)
text: chart now : centre line follows fitted trend, period period. Limits move centre line. Violations points depart expected trajectory, just constant baseline. Phase changes mark candidate inflection points: places trend shifted (new wave, containment measure taking effect, vaccination campaign starting).
code: fit <- shewhart_regression(   cvd_recife,   value      = new_deaths,   index      = .t,   model      = "loglog",   phase_rule = "we_seven_same",   # legacy WE rule used in the original analysis   rules      = c("nelson_1_beyond_3s", "we_seven_same") ) broom::glance(fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 8 #>   type        n phase sigma_hat sigma_method n_violations n_rules pct_violations #>   <chr>   <int> <chr>     <dbl> <chr>               <int>   <int>          <dbl> #> 1 regres…   279 phas…      3.47 mr                     10       2         0.0358 length(fit$fits)        # number of phases #> [1] 9 autoplot(fit)

title: Case study: epidemiological monitoring (COVID-19, Recife)
text: model = "loglog" choice applies transform log⁡(log⁡(y/α+1)+1)\log(\log(y/\alpha + 1) + 1) response fitting linear trend. aggressive log — stabilises variance right-skewed, heavy-tailed counts. COVID mortality counts, day--day variability scales sharply level, log-log scale gave cleanest residuals original analysis. Run shewhart_box_cox() series let data choose: maximiser near 0, take logs. near 0.5, log-log scale reasonable approximation. values 0 1, Box-Cox transformation range defensible choice.
code: shewhart_box_cox(cvd_recife$new_deaths + 1)$lambda_hat #> [1] 0

title: Case study: epidemiological monitoring (COVID-19, Recife)
text: regression chart applied time-series counts pushes several assumptions: Independence. Daily mortality counts autocorrelated (person dying tomorrow versus today rare; serially correlated -reporting reporting delays common). Use shewhart_diagnostics() inspect residual ACF; autocorrelation strong, consider ARIMA-based monitoring scheme instead (topic future package release). Right model. piecewise-linear log-scale fit heuristic. principled epidemic-monitoring approaches use SEIR-type models, renewal equation, compartmental Bayesian filters (Cori et al. 2013; Flaxman et al. 2020). package’s regression chart best understood signal-detection layer top whatever forecasting model one uses. Reporting heterogeneity. Counts “deaths today” usually reflect reports received today, deaths occurring today. Lagged corrections produce sawtooth patterns look like violations administrative.

title: Getting started with shewhartr
text: control chart serves two distinct purposes (Woodall 2000): Phase — retrospective analysis historical data, identify --control points arrive trustworthy -control limits. Phase II — prospective monitoring new data established limits. package draws line code via calibrate() monitor(): See vignette phase1-phase2 full discussion.
code: # Suppose the first 60 bottles are our calibration baseline, # and the next 40 are new data we want to monitor. baseline <- bottle_fill[1:60, ] new_obs  <- bottle_fill[61:100, ]  calib  <- calibrate(baseline, value = ml, index = observation,                     chart = "i_mr") alarms <- monitor(new_obs, calib) alarms$violations #> # A tibble: 0 × 5 #> # ℹ 5 variables: position <int>, rule <chr>, description <chr>, value <dbl>, #> #   severity <chr>

what: When the Shewhart chart is too slow
title: Memory-based charts: EWMA and CUSUM
text: Shewhart chart looks one observation time. point compared centre line 3-sigma limits, ’s — chart memory came (runs rules add little, little). makes Shewhart charts excellent catching large shifts moment happen, fairly slow catching small persistent shifts become visible data time drift. EWMA CUSUM accumulate information across observations, trading slightly less direct interpretation substantially better sensitivity small shifts. 0.5-sigma shift, -MR chart often fails signal within 80 observations, EWMA CUSUM typically catch within points change.
code: # A 0.5-sigma sustained shift after observation 40 set.seed(2026) n      <- 80 shift  <- c(rnorm(40, mean = 0, sd = 1),             rnorm(40, mean = 0.5, sd = 1)) df     <- tibble::tibble(t = seq_len(n), y = shift)  imr  <- shewhart_i_mr(df,  value = y, index = t) ewma <- shewhart_ewma(df,  value = y, index = t) cusum <- shewhart_cusum(df, value = y, index = t)  # Position of the first alarm under each chart first_alarm <- function(fit) {   hits <- which(fit$augmented$.flag_any)   if (length(hits) == 0L) NA_integer_ else min(hits) } tibble::tibble(   chart  = c("I-MR", "EWMA", "CUSUM"),   alarm  = c(first_alarm(imr), first_alarm(ewma), first_alarm(cusum)) ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   chart alarm #>   <chr> <int> #> 1 I-MR     10 #> 2 EWMA     NA #> 3 CUSUM    15

title: Memory-based charts: EWMA and CUSUM
text: EWMA statistic recursive convex combination: zi=λxi+(1−λ)zi−1,z0=μ.z_i = \lambda x_i + (1 - \lambda) z_{-1}, \qquad z_0 = \mu. lambda close 1 chart behaves like Shewhart chart (little memory). lambda close 0 averages long history (heavy memory, slow sensitive). classical default lambda = 0.2, L = 2.7 gives ARL_0 ≈ 370 well matched detecting shifts 0.5 1 sigma (Lucas & Saccucci 1990).  control limits EWMA chart time-varying default. start narrow widen asymptotic value L · σ · sqrt(λ / (2 - λ)) recursion warms . correct probability-symmetric calibration; setting steady_state = TRUE flattens asymptotic value (common simplification long enough baseline).
code: fit <- shewhart_ewma(df, value = y, index = t,                      lambda = 0.2, L = 2.7) fit #>  #> ── Shewhart chart ewma ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Observations / subgroups: 80 #> • Phase: "phase_1" #> • Sigma estimate ("mr"): 1.014 #>  #>  #> ── Control limits ── #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   chart line            value #>   <chr> <chr>           <dbl> #> 1 EWMA  CL              0.165 #> 2 EWMA  UCL_asymptotic  1.08  #> 3 EWMA  LCL_asymptotic -0.748 #> ── Rule violations ── #>  #> ✔ No violations across 1 rule: "nelson_1_beyond_3s". autoplot(fit) shewhart_ewma(df, value = y, index = t, steady_state = TRUE) |>   autoplot()

title: Memory-based charts: EWMA and CUSUM
text: tabular CUSUM keeps two non-negative running totals, one upward drift one downward drift: Ci+=max⁡(0,Ci−1++(xi−μ)−kσ) C^+_i = \max\!\left(0,\; C^+_{-1} + (x_i - \mu) - k\sigma\right) Ci−=max⁡(0,Ci−1−−(xi−μ)−kσ) C^-_i = \max\!\left(0,\; C^-_{-1} - (x_i - \mu) - k\sigma\right) alarm fires either crosses decision interval h · sigma. reference value k typically half smallest shift size chart sensitive (k = 0.5 shifts 1·σ); h controls false-alarm rate.  plot draws C+ positive bars (potential upward drift) C- negative bars (potential downward drift), symmetric decision interval dashed red. CUSUM’s main charm interpretability signals: bar’s height tells much accumulated deviation triggered alarm.
code: fit <- shewhart_cusum(df, value = y, index = t, k = 0.5, h = 4) fit #>  #> ── Shewhart chart cusum ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Observations / subgroups: 80 #> • Phase: "phase_1" #> • Sigma estimate ("mr"): 1.014 #>  #>  #> ── Control limits ── #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   chart line    value #>   <chr> <chr>   <dbl> #> 1 CUSUM h_upper  4.05 #> 2 CUSUM h_lower -4.05 #> ── Rule violations ── #>  #> ! 1 violation across 1 rule. #> cusum_decision: 1 hit. autoplot(fit)

title: Memory-based charts: EWMA and CUSUM
text: Hawkins & Olwell (1998), Table 3.1, gives ARL profiles k = 0.5 (common choice): default h = 4 appropriate diagnostic monitoring. production chart matched typical Shewhart ARL_0 = 370, raise h 4.77.

title: Memory-based charts: EWMA and CUSUM

what: When univariate charts are not enough
title: Multivariate charts: Hotelling T²
text: Suppose running chemical reactor three process variables — temperature, pressure, flow — mechanically coupled. normal operation move together: temperature rises, pressure rises . failure breaks coupling (say, stuck pressure sensor reads near mean temperature drifts) leaves marginal distribution variable inside 3-sigma limits, joint distribution clearly . Three Shewhart charts tell nothing happened. textbook case multivariate chart: informative signal lives correlation structure, one marginal. glance variable independently shows nothing dramatic:   Hotelling chart catches fault:
code: # Correlated baseline — temperature and pressure track together set.seed(2026) n     <- 80 Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.92, 0.92, 1), 2, 2) Z     <- MASS::mvrnorm(n, mu = c(0, 0), Sigma = Sigma)  # A "stuck pressure" fault: temperature still varies, pressure stays put faulty <- cbind(temp = rnorm(20, 0, 1), pressure = rnorm(20, 0, 0.05))  reactor <- tibble::tibble(   hour     = 1:100,   temp     = c(Z[, 1], faulty[, 1]),   pressure = c(Z[, 2], faulty[, 2]) ) shewhart_i_mr(reactor, value = temp,     index = hour) |> autoplot() #> Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_line()`). #> Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). shewhart_i_mr(reactor, value = pressure, index = hour) |> autoplot() #> Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_line()`). #> Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). fit <- shewhart_hotelling(reactor, vars = c(temp, pressure),                           index = hour) fit #>  #> ── Shewhart chart hotelling ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Observations / subgroups: 100 #> • Phase: "phase_1" #> • Sigma estimate ("hotelling"): NA #>  #>  #> ── Control limits ── #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   chart line  value #>   <chr> <chr> <dbl> #> 1 T2    UCL    11.3 #> ── Rule violations ── #>  #> ! 2 violations across 1 rule. #> hotelling_ucl: 2 hits. autoplot(fit)

title: Multivariate charts: Hotelling T²
text: Hotelling T² statistic squared Mahalanobis distance observation joint mean, scaled inverse covariance: Ti2=(xi−x‾)⊤S−1(xi−x‾)T^2_i = (x_i - \bar x)^\top S^{-1} (x_i - \bar x) collapses p-variate problem single scalar, UCL chosen null (process control, multivariate normal) false-alarm rate per observation alpha. default alpha = 0.0027 matches conventional Shewhart 3-sigma rate. Two flavours, picked automatically constructor: Individual observations (subgroup = NULL, default). row one observation. Sigma estimated row vectors. Subgrouped observations (subgroup = column). Rows sharing value subgroup form one subgroup, T² computed subgroup means pooled within-subgroup covariance. flavour, Phase limit (retrospective evaluation -control assumption) Phase II limit (prospective monitoring new data calibration) come different exact distributions; pass phase = "phase_2" wider Phase II UCL.

title: Multivariate charts: Hotelling T²
text: shewhart_hotelling() augments output contribution column per variable: marginal increase T² attributable variable. chart fires, contribution columns point responsible variable(s). reactor example, observations hour 80 typically bulk T² value coming .contrib_pressure — chart correctly fingerprinting stuck pressure sensor culprit.
code: fit$augmented |>   filter(.flag_signal) |>   select(hour, .t2, .upper, starts_with(".contrib_")) |>   head(5) #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>    hour   .t2 .upper .contrib_temp .contrib_pressure #>   <int> <dbl>  <dbl>         <dbl>             <dbl> #> 1    15  12.4   11.3          1.22              7.74 #> 2    97  11.8   11.3         11.8               8.27

title: Multivariate charts: Hotelling T²
text: calibrate() / monitor() workflow used univariate charts works multivariate chart :  monitor() reuses Phase mean vector inverse covariance (stored calibration object) applies appropriate Phase II UCL — strict separation estimation monitoring, matching rest package’s Phase / Phase II story.
code: set.seed(7) in_control <- as.data.frame(MASS::mvrnorm(120, c(0, 0), Sigma)) names(in_control) <- c("temp", "pressure") calib <- calibrate(in_control, vars = c(temp, pressure),                    chart = "hotelling")  # Fresh data — pressure-sensor fault for the last 10 readings new_data <- as.data.frame(MASS::mvrnorm(40, c(0, 0), Sigma)) names(new_data) <- c("temp", "pressure") new_data$pressure[31:40] <- rnorm(10, 0, 0.05)  mon <- monitor(new_data, calib) sum(mon$augmented$.flag_signal) #> [1] 3 autoplot(mon)

what: When not to reach for a Hotelling chart
title: Multivariate charts: Hotelling T²
text: multivariate chart free upgrade univariate charts. Three reasons sometimes wrong tool: Diagnosis harder. Univariate charts immediately tell variable drifted; Hotelling chart needs contribution decomposition point culprit. shifts affect one variable, matched univariate chart usually lower ARL_1. Sample-size hunger. Estimating p × p covariance well needs roughly 5 p 10 p observations per chart parameter. p = 5 variables ~50 observations just characterise -control state. sparse data, multivariate chart noise generator. Model assumptions. exact UCL calibrated joint normality. Multivariate non-normality, especially heavy tails, inflates false-alarm rate. Check shewhart_diagnostics() per variable committing. common production pattern: run univariate Shewhart EWMA charts every variable direct diagnosis, Hotelling chart top catch correlation-breaking faults univariate charts miss.

title: Phase I and Phase II
text: trim_outliers = TRUE enables iterative trimming: observation violates rules removed limits recomputed. procedure described Montgomery (2019), Section 6.2.3.
code: calib <- calibrate(   baseline,   value         = ml,   index         = observation,   chart         = "i_mr",   trim_outliers = TRUE ) calib #>  #> ── Shewhart chart I-MR (individuals & moving range) ──────────────────────────── #> • Observations / subgroups: 60 #> • Phase: "phase_1" #> • Sigma estimate ("mr"): 1.288 #>  #>  #> ── Control limits ── #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   chart line   value #>   <chr> <chr>  <dbl> #> 1 I     CL    500.   #> 2 I     UCL   504.   #> 3 I     LCL   496.   #> 4 MR    CL      1.45 #> 5 MR    UCL     4.75 #> 6 MR    LCL     0 #> ── Rule violations ── #>  #> ✔ No violations across 2 rules: "nelson_1_beyond_3s" and "nelson_2_nine_same". calib$phase #> [1] "phase_1" calib$n          # potentially less than nrow(baseline) if trimming dropped points #> [1] 60 broom::tidy(calib) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   chart line   value #>   <chr> <chr>  <dbl> #> 1 I     CL    500.   #> 2 I     UCL   504.   #> 3 I     LCL   496.   #> 4 MR    CL      1.45 #> 5 MR    UCL     4.75 #> 6 MR    LCL     0

title: Phase I and Phase II
text: monitor() re-estimate limits. propagates calibrated limits new data, applies rule set, returns fresh chart object whose phase slot "phase_2". can plot monitored series exactly way:
code: alarms <- monitor(new_obs, calib) alarms$phase #> [1] "phase_2" alarms$violations #> # A tibble: 0 × 5 #> # ℹ 5 variables: position <int>, rule <chr>, description <chr>, value <dbl>, #> #   severity <chr> autoplot(alarms)

title: Phase I and Phase II
text: Suppose baseline contains single contaminated observation — say, time operator forgot recalibrate scale. include , moving range inflated, sigma overestimated, future alarms lenient. trim step iteratively removes contamination calibration data. can disable trimming don’t trust automatic outlier removal: general principle (Tukey 1977): analyst look data trusting automated calibration. Use shewhart_diagnostics() runs-violation table interrogate baseline declaring “control”.
code: calib_no_trim <- calibrate(baseline, value = ml,                            chart = "i_mr",                            trim_outliers = FALSE) calib_no_trim$sigma_hat #> [1] 1.288322 calib$sigma_hat       # potentially smaller after trimming #> [1] 1.288322

title: Phase I and Phase II
text: Phase / Phase II distinction consequential : process evolved (operators, materials, environment) since historical baseline collected. Limits estimated old data may misfit new operating conditions. small sustained shift present. Re-estimating limits monitoring run masks slow drifts. compare different time periods. Without fixed limits, comparison two estimates rather single reference.

title: Regression-based control charts
text: temperature_drift 200 minutes sensor readings curing oven. truth slow linear drift superimposed periodic component plus noise.  chart’s centre line follows fitted line, limits ±3σ̂R\pm 3\hat{\sigma}_R σ̂R\hat{\sigma}_R estimated moving range residuals.
code: fit <- shewhart_regression(   temperature_drift,   value = temp_c,   index = minute,   model = "linear" ) broom::glance(fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 8 #>   type        n phase sigma_hat sigma_method n_violations n_rules pct_violations #>   <chr>   <int> <chr>     <dbl> <chr>               <int>   <int>          <dbl> #> 1 regres…   200 phas…     0.366 mr                     11       2          0.055 autoplot(fit)

title: Regression-based control charts
text: subtlety: residuals regression fit correlated ordinary least squares, even model correctly specified (adjacent residuals share influence fitted slope). classical MR¯/d2\overline{\mathrm{MR}}/d_2 estimator partially absorbs , short series autocorrelated noise recommend checking residuals first via shewhart_diagnostics(): ACF panel shows non-trivial autocorrelation, consider modelling explicitly (wider topic; see Box, Jenkins & Reinsel 2008) relying chart.
code: shewhart_diagnostics(fit)    # residuals~fitted, Q-Q, ACF, MR, histogram

title: Regression-based control charts
text: shewhart_regression() can split series phases automatically: runs rule fires (default Nelson 2 — nine consecutive points side fitted curve), new phase begins model re-fit. generalises original v0.1.x behaviour cleaner, configurable rule: phase_rule argument accepts rule shewhart_rules_available(). legacy 7-points rule v0.1.x still available "we_seven_same": trade-straightforward. Nelson 2 (9 side), -control ARL 256 — false phase changes rare. 7-rule, ARL_0 64 — phase changes detected faster higher false-alarm cost. See arl-simulation vignette quantitative comparison.
code: set.seed(1) trended <- tibble::tibble(   t = 1:120,   y = c( 1:60 * 0.5  + rnorm(60, sd = 0.5),     # phase 1          30 + 1:60 * 0.1 + rnorm(60, sd = 0.5)) # phase 2: shift + slowdown )  fit <- shewhart_regression(trended, value = y, index = t,                            model = "linear",                            phase_rule = "nelson_2_nine_same") broom::glance(fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 8 #>   type        n phase sigma_hat sigma_method n_violations n_rules pct_violations #>   <chr>   <int> <chr>     <dbl> <chr>               <int>   <int>          <dbl> #> 1 regres…   120 phas…     0.481 mr                      7       2         0.0583 length(fit$fits)    # number of phases detected #> [1] 7 fit_legacy <- shewhart_regression(trended, value = y, index = t,                                   model = "linear",                                   phase_rule = "we_seven_same") length(fit_legacy$fits) #> [1] 9 autoplot(fit)            # Nelson 2 — usually 1–2 phases autoplot(fit_legacy)     # WE 7 — typically more phases on the same data

what: A worked example with many phases and visible violations
title: Regression-based control charts
text: COVID-19 mortality series Recife textbook trended process: long, irregular climb, peak, slow decline. single regression line legacy we_seven_same rule (one original SBPO 2020 paper used), chart partitions series phases, fits local trend , flags days observed value departs sharply local trend.  shaded band phase. dashed lines phase’s 3-sigma limits, solid line regression centre line phase. Points highlighted firebrick days flagged rule set departing local trend — days motivated investigation original analysis. violations table tells exactly days fired rule:
code: fit_recife <- shewhart_regression(   cvd_recife,   value      = new_deaths,   index      = .t,   model      = "loglog",   phase_rule = "we_seven_same",   rules      = c("nelson_1_beyond_3s", "we_seven_same"),   lower_bound = 0          # death counts can't go negative )  length(fit_recife$fits)              # phases detected #> [1] 9 nrow(fit_recife$violations)          # individual flagged observations #> [1] 10 autoplot(fit_recife) head(fit_recife$violations, 8) #> # A tibble: 8 × 5 #>   position rule               description            value severity       #>      <int> <chr>              <chr>                  <int> <chr>          #> 1       52 nelson_1_beyond_3s 1 point beyond 3 sigma    49 out_of_control #> 2       61 nelson_1_beyond_3s 1 point beyond 3 sigma    65 out_of_control #> 3       86 nelson_1_beyond_3s 1 point beyond 3 sigma    48 out_of_control #> 4      102 nelson_1_beyond_3s 1 point beyond 3 sigma    27 out_of_control #> 5      104 nelson_1_beyond_3s 1 point beyond 3 sigma    18 out_of_control #> 6      111 nelson_1_beyond_3s 1 point beyond 3 sigma    23 out_of_control #> 7      117 nelson_1_beyond_3s 1 point beyond 3 sigma    15 out_of_control #> 8      181 nelson_1_beyond_3s 1 point beyond 3 sigma    17 out_of_control

text: mean moving range sensitive outliers. Three robust alternatives built : biweight Tukey’s M-estimator redescending influence function (Tukey 1977; Mosteller & Tukey 1977). Use suspect small fraction contaminated observations don’t want inflate sigma.
code: fit_med <- shewhart_i_mr(bottle_fill, value = ml,                          sigma_method = "median_mr") fit_bw  <- shewhart_i_mr(bottle_fill, value = ml,                          sigma_method = "biweight") fit_sd  <- shewhart_i_mr(bottle_fill, value = ml,                          sigma_method = "sd")  dplyr::bind_rows(   broom::glance(fit)     |> mutate(method = "mr (default)"),   broom::glance(fit_med) |> mutate(method = "median_mr"),   broom::glance(fit_bw)  |> mutate(method = "biweight"),   broom::glance(fit_sd)  |> mutate(method = "sd") ) |>   select(method, sigma_hat, n_violations) #> # A tibble: 4 × 3 #>   method       sigma_hat n_violations #>   <chr>            <dbl>        <int> #> 1 mr (default)      1.18            0 #> 2 median_mr         1.18            0 #> 3 biweight          1.23            0 #> 4 sd                1.22            0

title: Variables charts: I-MR, Xbar-R, Xbar-S
text: tablet_weight records 25 batches 5 tablets . Xbar limits use constant A2(5)=0.577A_2(5) = 0.577; R limits use D3(5)=0D_3(5) = 0 D4(5)=2.114D_4(5) = 2.114. constants come Montgomery (2019), Appendix VI, exposed via shewhart_constants(): shewhart_xbar_r() insists equal subgroup sizes (otherwise constants don’t apply uniformly). unequal sizes use shewhart_xbar_s() sigma_method = "pooled_sd".

title: Variables charts: I-MR, Xbar-R, Xbar-S
text: S chart uses bias-corrected sample standard deviation. Sigma estimated σ̂=S‾c4(n),c4(n)=2n−1⋅Γ(n/2)Γ((n−1)/2). \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{S}}{c_4(n)}, \qquad c_4(n) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{n-1}} \cdot \frac{\Gamma(n/2)}{\Gamma((n-1)/2)}. variable subgroup sizes:
code: # Same data, but using S instead of R (preferred for n > 10) n_per <- 5 df <- tablet_weight |>   group_by(subgroup) |>   filter(n() == n_per) |>     # in case of any incomplete batches   ungroup()  fit_xbs <- shewhart_xbar_s(df, value = weight, subgroup = subgroup) broom::glance(fit_xbs) #> # A tibble: 1 × 8 #>   type       n phase  sigma_hat sigma_method n_violations n_rules pct_violations #>   <chr>  <int> <chr>      <dbl> <chr>               <int>   <int>          <dbl> #> 1 xbar_s    25 phase…      1.49 sbar                    2       2           0.08 fit_xbs_pooled <- shewhart_xbar_s(df, value = weight, subgroup = subgroup,                                   sigma_method = "pooled_sd")

title: Variables charts: I-MR, Xbar-R, Xbar-S
text: Every variables chart returns shewhart_chart object following per-observation columns (broom::augment()): .value — plotted statistic (individual, Xbar, etc.) .center, .sigma, .upper, .lower — control-line series two-panel charts, additional .mr*, .r*, .s* columns lower panel .flag_<rule> .flag_any — TRUE/FALSE runs rule configured rules argument controls rules flagged. default, Nelson 1 (point beyond 3 sigma) Nelson 2 (9 points side) checked, recommend keeping least two. Add : additional rule increases chart’s sensitivity cost shorter -control ARL. See vignette("arl-simulation") quantify trade-.
code: fit_full <- shewhart_i_mr(bottle_fill, value = ml,                           rules = c("nelson_1_beyond_3s",                                     "nelson_2_nine_same",                                     "nelson_3_six_trend",                                     "nelson_5_two_of_three",                                     "nelson_6_four_of_five")) fit_full$violations #> # A tibble: 0 × 5 #> # ℹ 5 variables: position <int>, rule <chr>, description <chr>, value <dbl>, #> #   severity <chr>

what: Statistical Process Control with Tidyverse-Native Workflows
title: Statistical Process Control with Tidyverse-Native Workflows
text: shewhartr tidyverse-native toolkit Statistical Process Control (SPC). implements classical Shewhart chart family — variables (-MR, Xbar-R, Xbar-S) attributes (p, np, c, u) — alongside flagship regression-based control chart processes trend, stationarity strong assumption make. package built around small set design choices: Tidy default. Every constructor takes data first, supports tidy-eval column references, returns S3 object integrates broom via tidy(), glance() augment(). Diagnostics box. Average Run Length (ARL) Monte Carlo simulation, eight Nelson runs rules, Box-Cox guidance, Tukey-style residual panel first-class citizens. Phase / Phase II. Explicit calibrate() monitor() functions make distinction estimation prospective monitoring impossible confuse, following Woodall (2000). Multilingual plots. Pass locale = "pt" ("es", "fr") chart titles, axis labels, legends translated. Statistically honest counts. c u charts accept limits = "poisson" exact Poisson quantile limits, instead normal approximation breaks small means.

title: Statistical Process Control with Tidyverse-Native Workflows
text: flagship chart trended processes splits series phases runs rule fires, fits local model , flags points depart local trend. example uses COVID-19 mortality series Recife (cvd_recife) original analysis settings Ferraz et al. (2020):  shaded band one phase, solid line local regression centre, dashed lines phase’s 3-sigma limits, firebrick points days flagged rule set departing local trend.
code: fit <- shewhart_regression(   cvd_recife,   value       = new_deaths,   index       = .t,   model       = "loglog",   phase_rule  = "we_seven_same",   rules       = c("nelson_1_beyond_3s", "we_seven_same"),   lower_bound = 0 )  length(fit$fits)              # number of phases detected nrow(fit$violations)          # individual flagged observations autoplot(fit)

title: Statistical Process Control with Tidyverse-Native Workflows
text: Shewhart chart serves two different purposes easy conflate. Phase retrospective: take historical data, identify --control points, eliminate assignable causes, arrive trustworthy estimates process mean variability. Phase II prospective: take estimated limits apply new data, signalling alarms something departs established baseline. package draws line code:
code: # Phase I: estimate limits from a clean baseline calib <- calibrate(historical_data, value = y,                    chart = "i_mr", trim_outliers = TRUE)  # Phase II: apply the limits to new data alarms <- monitor(new_observations, calib) alarms$violations

Plot: autoplot() (ggplot2) as_plotly() (interactive, plotly Suggests). Tidy: broom::tidy(), glance(), augment(). Phase II: calibrate() produces Phase chart whose limits frozen monitor() prospective monitoring.

code: # \donttest{ set.seed(42) n <- 50 x <- seq(1, 10, length.out = n) d <- rep(c(0, 1), each = n / 2) y <- 100 * exp(-2 * exp(-0.3 * x)) + 20 * d + rnorm(n, 0, 3) df <- data.frame(x = x, y = y, dummy = d)  fit <- nls(y ~ SSgompertzDummy(x, dummy, Asym, b2, b3, Beta), data = df) summary(fit) #>  #> Formula: y ~ SSgompertzDummy(x, dummy, Asym, b2, b3, Beta) #>  #> Parameters: #>       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)     #> Asym 103.94202    5.56568   18.68  < 2e-16 *** #> b2     1.86485    0.09362   19.92  < 2e-16 *** #> b3     0.26619    0.02420   11.00 1.81e-14 *** #> Beta  19.33285    2.08111    9.29 4.02e-12 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> Residual standard error: 3.419 on 46 degrees of freedom #>  #> Number of iterations to convergence: 4  #> Achieved convergence tolerance: 2.338e-07 #>  # }

Number simulation replicates per shift. Default: 5000. max_run Integer. Maximum run length truncation (censored alarm). Default: 1000. sigma Numeric. -control sigma. title: Estimate Average Run Length via Monte Carlo simulation — shewhart_arl

Defaults mean(value). sigma Numeric. Process sigma. Defaults MR_bar / 1.128. lambda Numeric (0, 1]. Smoothing constant. Default 0.2. Smaller lambda = memory, sensitive small shifts. L Numeric. Use asymptotic (constant) limits instead time-varying ones? rules Character vector runs rules flag. Defaults Nelson 1 — EWMA's limits already encode diagnostic power higher-order Nelson rules designed autocorrelated statistics. locale One "en", "pt", "es", "fr". verbose Logical. Use chart variables genuinely co-vary — classical example chemical process temperature, pressure flow rate mechanically coupled, fault breaks coupling moves joint distribution possibly stays inside marginal limit.

what: shewhartr: Statistical Process Control with Tidyverse-Native Workflows — shewhartr-package
title: shewhartr: Statistical Process Control with Tidyverse-Native Workflows — shewhartr-package
text: shewhartr package brings classical Statistical Process Control (SPC) methodology modern, tidy-friendly R workflow. implements full family classical Shewhart control charts (-MR, Xbar-R, Xbar-S, p, np, c, u), regression-based charts processes trend, runs tests (Nelson 1-8), Average Run Length (ARL) simulation, process capability indices, Box-Cox guidance, explicit Phase / Phase II workflow.

title: shewhartr: Statistical Process Control with Tidyverse-Native Workflows — shewhartr-package
text: Tidyverse-native constructors take data first argument, support tidy evaluation column references via {{ }}, accept .argument group-aware behaviour makes sense. Object-oriented Every chart returns S3 object class shewhart_chart (specific subclass like shewhart_i_mr) supporting print(), summary(), plot(), autoplot(), tidy(), glance() augment(). Diagnostic-rich Every chart can passed shewhart_runs() (configurable rule sets), shewhart_arl() (Monte Carlo ARL), shewhart_diagnostics() (Tukey-style residual panel) shewhart_capability() (Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk bootstrap CI). Statistically honest Counts charted exact Poisson limits requested (limits = "poisson"), per Box's advice model right distribution rather transform. Robust alternatives charts accept sigma_method = "biweight" Tukey-style robust scale estimation alternative classical moving range. Internationalisation Plot labels informative messages accept locale argument ("en", "pt", "es", "fr").

title: shewhartr: Statistical Process Control with Tidyverse-Native Workflows — shewhartr-package
text: Classical charts (variables) shewhart_i_mr(), shewhart_xbar_r(), shewhart_xbar_s(). Classical charts (attributes) shewhart_p(), shewhart_np(), shewhart_c(), shewhart_u(). Regression-based chart shewhart_regression() trended processes, optional automatic phase detection via runs test. Diagnostics & methodology shewhart_runs(), shewhart_arl(), shewhart_diagnostics(), shewhart_box_cox(), shewhart_capability(), calibrate(), monitor().

title: shewhartr 1.3.0 (development version)
text: multivariate side package now natural pair memory-based charts. follow calibrate() / monitor() Phase / Phase II workflow. Table 3 (ARL_0 ~ 200). shewhart_mcusum() — Multivariate CUSUM (Crosier 1988). multivariate analogue shewhart_cusum(). Uses Crosier’s shrinkage operator: step cumulative vector shrunk towards zero k / C_i C_i Mahalanobis norm; norm falls k, cumulative vector resets zero. Decision interval h calibrated lookup Crosier (1988) Table 1 k = 0.5, ARL_0 ~ 200, p = 2..10. Phase II monitoring continues calibration’s final S vector (Crosier 1988 §5).

title: shewhartr 1.3.0 (development version)
text: Every autoplot() method now uses shewhart_palette() shewhart_theme(), plots chart family share editorial look (-white surface, single horizontal grid, bold left-aligned title, sequential phase palette, hollow firebrick rings --control points). --control marks centralised new internal violation_layers() helper, sized halo 1.7 / ring 1.4 / stroke 0.7. Regression chart legend title now plural bold (“Phases” / “Fases”) uses localised Phase 0, Phase 1, … labels via phase_n new legend_phases locale entries. arl-simulation vignette’s hand-rolled chart using theme_minimal() default ggplot2 colours; now uses shewhart_theme() package palette matches rest.

title: shewhartr 1.3.0 (development version)
text: Three U+2014 em-dash characters R/autoplot.R (two comments, one string literal) replaced ASCII alternatives — escape R CMD check longer warns non-ASCII source.

title: shewhartr 1.1.0
text: shewhart_hotelling() package’s first multivariate chart — Hotelling T² chart jointly monitoring p > 1 correlated variables. individual observations (subgroup = NULL) subgrouped data supported, appropriate exact Phase limits (Beta individuals, F subgroups) slightly wider Phase II limits derived Tracy, Young & Mason (1992) Montgomery (2019, Chapter 11). implementation follows Mason & Young (2002). augmented tibble carries T² statistic per row, decomposition variable (contribution variable alarm — useful T² signals univariate chart ), logical flag appropriate chart-level UCL. Vignette multivariate-charts walks standard worked example: chemical process three correlated quality characteristics, showing multivariate chart catches correlation-breaking shift three univariate charts miss.

title: shewhartr 1.0.0
text: release comprehensive reposition package. original (v0.1.x, distributed Shewhart) COVID-focused provided single function family regression-based control charts. v1.0.0 full general-purpose SPC toolkit preserving regression-chart speciality motivated package. Rename. part reposition, package renamed Shewhart → shewhartr follow modern lowercase R-package conventions free name “Shewhart” methodology text documentation. Update existing code library(shewhartr) (formerly library(Shewhart)). API substantially redesigned. Existing scripts written v0.1.x run unchanged.

title: shewhartr 1.0.0
text: package now general-purpose SPC toolkit. COVID-19 use case preserved vignette case study (covid-recife), organising principle. package competes space qcc qicharts2, emphasises five differentiators: tidyverse-native API, broom integration, regression-based charts first-class citizen, embedded methodology (ARL, Box-Cox, runs tests), explicit Phase / Phase II workflow.

title: shewhartr 1.0.0
text: Variables: shewhart_i_mr(), shewhart_xbar_r(), shewhart_xbar_s(). Attributes: shewhart_p(), shewhart_np(), shewhart_c(), shewhart_u(). c u charts accept limits = "poisson" exact Poisson quantile limits rather normal approximation. flagship shewhart_regression() replaces old shewhart() function cleaner API, extensible model menu (auto, linear, log, loglog, gompertz, logistic, plus user formulas), automatic phase detection via configurable runs rules, proper handling irregular time grids. Memory-based charts: shewhart_ewma() (Roberts 1959) Exponentially Weighted Moving Average chart, time-varying steady-state limits, shewhart_cusum() (Page 1954) two-sided tabular CUSUM chart configurable reference value k decision interval h. fit S3 / broom / autoplot pipeline classical charts.

title: shewhartr 1.0.0
text: shewhart_runs() implements eight Nelson rules (1984, 1985) plus Western Electric “7 row” variant backward compatibility. Rule sets user-configurable every chart. shewhart_arl() performs Monte Carlo Average Run Length simulation arbitrary rule combinations. shewhart_box_cox() returns profile log-likelihood, optimal lambda, 95% CI Box & Cox (1964) tradition. shewhart_diagnostics() produces five-panel Tukey-style residual diagnostic display. shewhart_capability() computes Cp/Cpk/Pp/Ppk bootstrap confidence intervals. calibrate() monitor() provide explicit Phase / Phase II workflow.

title: shewhartr 1.0.0
text: charts return S3 object class shewhart_chart specific subclass. print(), summary(), autoplot(), tidy(), glance() augment() methods provided every chart type.

title: shewhartr 1.0.0
text: user-facing chart functions accept locale argument ("en", "pt", "es", "fr") controls plot labels informative messages. Validation errors remain English facilitate cross-user debugging.

title: shewhartr 1.0.0
text: Depends collapsed: package longer depends entire tidyverse meta-package tibbletime/pals/scales. new Imports minimal explicit. plotly moved Suggests avoid pulling heavy dependency users don’t need interactive plots. input validation uses cli::cli_abort() / cli::cli_warn() multi-line, informative messages. .onAttach() longer prints banner. New data-raw/build_all.R script generates six synthetic datasets (tablet_weight, bottle_fill, pcb_solder, claims_p, temperature_drift, bacterial_growth). New documentation site built pkgdown https://castlaboratory.github.io/shewhartr/. Ten topical articles, including dedicated memory-based-charts vignette covering EWMA, CUSUM trade-offs versus Shewhart-style charts.

title: shewhartr 1.0.0
text: shewhart() (plotting function) removed. Use shewhart_regression() followed autoplot(). shewhart_fit() longer exported. Use shewhart_regression(). shewhart_model() longer exported. Use shewhart_regression(). shewhart_7points() longer exported. Use shewhart_runs(rule = "we_seven_same") behaviour, recommended shewhart_runs(rule = "nelson_2_nine_same"). default phase-detection rule now Nelson 2 (9 points side; ARL_0 ~ 256), legacy 7-points--side (ARL_0 ~ 64). Pass phase_rule = "we_seven_same" recover old behaviour. package banner printed .onAttach() removed. output column names follow broom-style leading . (.fitted, .upper, .lower, .flag_*).
